news_summary: Scientists have uncovered the giant sea predator Tylosaurus rex, shedding new light on ancient ocean ecosystems.
news_summary_fr: Des scientifiques ont mis au jour le Tylosaurus rex, un prédateur marin géant, apportant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur les écosystèmes océaniques d'autrefois.
news_summary_es: Los científicos han descubierto al Tylosaurus rex, un depredador marino gigante, lo que arroja nueva luz sobre los ecosistemas oceánicos de la antigüedad.

articles.title: Scientists discover giant sea predator Tylosaurus rex that terrorized ancient oceans
articles.summary: A colossal new sea predator named Tylosaurus rex has been identified from fossils found in Texas, revealing a brutal 43-foot-long hunter that ruled ancient oceans 80 million years ago. The discovery not only introduces one of the biggest mosasaurs ever known, but also shakes up long-standing ideas about how these marine reptiles evolved.
articles.content:
Scientists have identified a massive new species of mosasaur, a marine reptile that lived alongside the dinosaurs and dominated prehistoric oceans. The newly described predator, named Tylosaurus rex (or T. rex), measured up to 43 feet long and ranks among the largest mosasaurs ever discovered.

The research was led by scientists from the American Museum of Natural History, the Perot Museum of Nature and Science in Dallas, and Southern Methodist University. Fossils of the giant reptile were found mainly in northern Texas and date back about 80 million years.

"Everything is bigger in Texas and that includes the mosasaurs, apparently," said Amelia Zietlow, lead author of the study published in the Bulletin of the American Museum of Natural History.

Zietlow, now at the History Museum at the Castle in Wisconsin, began investigating the fossils while working as a Ph.D. student in comparative biology at the American Museum of Natural History's Richard Gilder Graduate School. Her research started after she noticed that one fossil in the museum's collection appeared to have been incorrectly identified as another species, Tylosaurus proriger.

Fossils Reveal a Different and More Powerful Mosasaur

After comparing the specimen with the original T. proriger fossil housed at Harvard University's Museum of Comparative Zoology, researchers realized the Texas fossils represented a different species altogether. More than a dozen similar fossils stored in museums were eventually linked to the newly identified predator.

The team found several differences between the two species. The new mosasaur was larger, possessed finely serrated teeth rarely seen in mosasaurs, and came from a different place and time period. Most T. proriger fossils have been found in Kansas and date to roughly 84 million years ago, while the new species primarily lived in what is now Texas about 80 million years ago.

Researchers chose the name Tylosaurus rex as a tribute to paleontologist John Thurmond. In the late 1960s, Thurmond recognized that giant tylosaurs from northeast Texas appeared unusually large and may have belonged to a separate species. He informally referred to them as "Tylosaurus thalassotyrannus," meaning "sea tyrant."

The holotype specimen for T. rex is currently displayed at the Perot Museum in Dallas. The fossil was originally discovered in 1979 near an artificial reservoir outside the city.

Evidence Suggests Violent Battles Between Sea Predators

Beyond its enormous size, researchers believe Tylosaurus rex was built for strength and aggression. The species had adaptations linked to especially powerful jaw and neck muscles, making it a formidable hunter.

"Besides being huge, roughly twice the length of the largest great white sharks, T. rex appeared to be a much meaner animal than other mosasaurs," said study co-author Ron Tykoski, vice-president of science and curator of vertebrate paleontology at the Perot Museum. "Through our study and examination of well-preserved fossils collected throughout the north Texas region, we have evidence of violence within this species to a degree not previously seen in other Tylosaurus specimens."

Scientists point to one famous specimen nicknamed "The Black Knight" as evidence of this violent behavior. The fossil, housed at the Perot Museum, is missing the tip of its snout and shows a fractured lower jaw. Researchers say the injuries were most likely caused by another member of the same species.

Other famous mosasaur fossils previously identified as T. proriger are now being reassigned to T. rex. These include "Bunker," a massive fossil displayed at the University of Kansas, and "Sophie," which is exhibited at the Yale Peabody Museum.

Discovery Challenges Longstanding Ideas About Mosasaur Evolution

The study also tackles a major issue in mosasaur research. Scientists noted that the main dataset used to study evolutionary relationships among mosasaurs has changed very little over the past three decades.

To address this, the team created a revised dataset and developed a new evolutionary framework for tylosaurs. Their findings suggest that many previous studies may need to be revisited.

"This discovery is not just about naming a new species," Zietlow said. "It highlights the need to revisit long-standing assumptions about mosasaur evolution and to modernize the tools we use to study these iconic marine reptiles."

Co-author Michael Polcyn from Southern Methodist University said the discovery expands understanding of both the animals themselves and the ancient ecosystems they inhabited.

"These findings reshape both the physical and evolutionary picture of mosasaurs, underscoring Texas as a key region for understanding ancient marine ecosystems and signaling a new era of research into the evolutionary history of these formidable predators."

Support for the research came in part from the National Science Foundation, the Dallas Paleontological Society, the Society of Systematic Biologists, the Richard Gilder Graduate School, the Gingrich Fund, and the Carter Fund.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery of a new giant sea predator species, Tylosaurus rex, which reshapes understanding of ancient marine ecosystems and mosasaur evolution. The discovery has broad scientific significance and advances paleontology knowledge with detailed fossil evidence and evolutionary insights.
articles.personality_title: Scientists identify giant sea predator Tylosaurus rex from Texas fossils
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mosasaurs were large marine reptiles that lived during the time of the dinosaurs, dominating the oceans about 80 million years ago. Scientists have studied their fossils for decades to understand their size, behavior, and evolution.

**What happened** – Researchers from several museums and universities discovered a new species of mosasaur called Tylosaurus rex based on fossils found in northern Texas. This predator was about 43 feet long, making it one of the largest mosasaurs ever found. The study began when a scientist noticed one fossil had been wrongly identified and, after comparing many fossils, they confirmed it was a new species. The fossils show that Tylosaurus rex was stronger and more aggressive than other mosasaurs, with sharp teeth and powerful jaws.

**Impact** – This discovery changes how scientists understand mosasaur evolution by showing that some giant species lived in Texas, not just other places like Kansas. It also reveals that these animals fought fiercely, based on fossil injuries. The finding leads to a new way of studying mosasaurs and their ancient ocean environments, highlighting Texas as an important area for paleontology.

**What's next step** – Scientists will use the new data and fossils to revise how mosasaurs are classified and how they evolved. They plan to study more fossils from Texas and other areas to learn about these sea predators’ lives and to update the tools used for studying ancient marine reptiles.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The discovery of Tylosaurus rex, a massive and fierce ancient sea predator from Texas, reshapes what we know about mosasaurs and their ocean world 80 million years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques identifient le grand prédateur marin Tylosaurus rex grâce à des fossiles du Texas
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mosasaures étaient de grands reptiles marins qui vivaient à l'époque des dinosaures, dominant les océans il y a environ 80 millions d'années. Les scientifiques étudient leurs fossiles depuis des décennies pour comprendre leur taille, leur comportement et leur évolution.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs de plusieurs musées et universités ont découvert une nouvelle espèce de mosasaure appelée Tylosaurus rex, basée sur des fossiles trouvés dans le nord du Texas. Ce prédateur mesurait environ 13 mètres de long, ce qui en fait l'un des plus grands mosasaures jamais découverts. L'étude a commencé lorsqu'une scientifique a remarqué qu'un fossile avait été mal identifié et, après avoir comparé plusieurs fossiles, ils ont confirmé qu'il s'agissait d'une nouvelle espèce. Les fossiles montrent que Tylosaurus rex était plus fort et plus agressif que les autres mosasaures, avec des dents tranchantes et une mâchoire puissante.

**Impact** – Cette découverte modifie la compréhension de l'évolution des mosasaures en montrant que certaines espèces géantes vivaient au Texas, et pas seulement dans des endroits comme le Kansas. Elle révèle aussi que ces animaux se battaient violemment, d'après les blessures sur les fossiles. Cette découverte ouvre une nouvelle façon d'étudier les mosasaures et leurs anciens environnements marins, soulignant l'importance du Texas en paléontologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont utiliser ces nouvelles données et fossiles pour revoir la classification et l'évolution des mosasaures. Ils prévoient d'étudier plus de fossiles du Texas et d'ailleurs pour mieux comprendre la vie de ces prédateurs marins et mettre à jour les méthodes d'étude des reptiles marins anciens.

**En une phrase** – La découverte du Tylosaurus rex, un énorme et féroce prédateur marin ancien du Texas, change notre connaissance des mosasaures et de leur monde océanique il y a 80 millions d'années.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos identifican al enorme depredador marino Tylosaurus rex a partir de fósiles en Texas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los mosasaurios fueron grandes reptiles marinos que vivieron en la época de los dinosaurios y dominaban los océanos hace unos 80 millones de años. Los científicos han estudiado sus fósiles durante décadas para entender su tamaño, comportamiento y evolución.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de varios museos y universidades descubrieron una nueva especie de mosasaurio llamada Tylosaurus rex basada en fósiles encontrados en el norte de Texas. Este depredador medía cerca de 13 metros, siendo uno de los mosasaurios más grandes conocidos. El estudio comenzó cuando una científica notó que un fósil había sido identificado incorrectamente y, al comparar muchos fósiles, confirmaron que era una especie nueva. Los fósiles muestran que Tylosaurus rex era más fuerte y agresivo que otros mosasaurios, con dientes afilados y mandíbulas poderosas.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento cambia cómo los científicos entienden la evolución de los mosasaurios al mostrar que algunas especies gigantes vivieron en Texas, no solo en lugares como Kansas. También revela que estos animales peleaban violentamente, según las heridas en los fósiles. El hallazgo ofrece una nueva forma de estudiar a los mosasaurios y sus antiguos ecosistemas marinos, destacando a Texas como un lugar clave para la paleontología.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos usarán los nuevos datos y fósiles para revisar la clasificación y evolución de los mosasaurios. Planean estudiar más fósiles de Texas y otras regiones para aprender más sobre la vida de estos depredadores marinos y actualizar las herramientas para estudiar reptiles marinos antiguos.

**Una frase clave** – El descubrimiento de Tylosaurus rex, un enorme y feroz depredador marino antiguo de Texas, cambia lo que sabemos sobre los mosasaurios y su mundo oceánico hace 80 millones de años.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a massive, powerful mosasaur with finely serrated teeth swimming through ancient Texan coastal waters, surrounded by fossilized bones embedded in the seabed and subtle silhouettes of rival mosasaurs hinting at fierce battles, all rendered in natural, muted earth and ocean colors.
